coredata.dc:title: Large-scale direct pyrolysis synthesis of excitation-independent carbon dots and analysis of ferric (III) ion sensing mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yongyu
Chen, Jianjun
Wang, Yaping
Li, Huiji
Yin, Jihong
Li, Mengyan
Wang, Ling
Sun, Haijie
Chen, Lingxia
coredata.dc:description: A one-step direct pyrolysis method without any post-treatment steps and a mass yield of up to 69% was realized for preparing N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The NCDs exhibit excellent excitation-independent blue fluorescence with quantum yield (QY) of 65.5%, and their luminescence mechanism was explored based on optical properties and structural composition, which mainly originates from surface state photoluminescence (PL). The NCDs display highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in tris-HCl buffer system, a good linear relationship between PL intensity ratios and Fe3+concentrations was obtained in concentration range of 0 to 400 μM with a low LOD of 0.703 μM. The formation of ferric hydroxide colloid is the main reason for the specific recognition of Fe3+ by NCDs, and buffer solution plays a key role in the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid and selective detection of Fe3+. The sensing mechanism is a dynamic quenching electron transfer process in the action of hydrogen bonding and colloidal adsorption. Three real water samples were applied to evaluate actual application of NCDs probe, satisfactory recoveries (93.5%–105.6%) indicate that NCDs has great practical potential for detection of metal ions in environmental water assays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCDs
Direct pyrolysis synthesis
Fe3+ detection
Sensing mechanism
Ferric hydroxide colloid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
Formation and PL mechanism of NCDs
Effect of storage conditions on selectivity and sensitivity
Reproducibility and tested stability of NCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Henan Provincial Science and Technology Research Project
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution histogram, (c) HRTEM image, (d) XRD pattern of NCDs.
XPS spectra of NCDs, (a) Survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and PLE of NCDs, and UV–Vis absorption of CA and EDA solution. Inset: the images of NCDs solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm (right), (b) PL emission spectra of NCDs excited by various wavelengths ranging from 290 to 430 nm with 20 nm increments.
(a) PL intensities of the solutions of solid NCDs stored at different temperatures for one week, (b) PL intensities of the solutions of solid NCDs stored for different times at 25℃.

(a) Effect of pH on PL intensity of NCDs solutions at λ em = 450 nm, (b) Effect of storage time on the PL intensity of NCDs solutions, (c) Effect of different oxidants on the PL spectra of NCDs solutions. Inset: photographs of NCDs solutions containing different oxidants under sunlight (up) and 365 nm (down), (d) Effect of NaCl concentrations on the PL intensity of NCDs solutions.

(a) Effect of various metal ions on the spectra of NCDs. Inset: digital photograph of NCDs containing different metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp. (b) Selectivity studies of NCDs solutions by using various metal ions (red column) and interference studies of various metal ions on Fe3+sensing (blue column).
(a) Emission spectra of NCDs at Fe3+ concentration range of 0–5000 μM. (b) Dependence of (F0-F)/F on the Fe3+ concentration (0–350 μM).

(a) Transient fluorescence spectroscopy of NCDs and NCDs–Fe3+ mixture (cFe3+ = 333 μM). (b) UV–Vis absorption of NCDs, Fe3+ and NCDs-Fe3+ system (blue) (cFe3+ = 333 μM), the calculated absorption value of NCDs and Fe3+ solution (green), and PL emission of NCDs, the inset is partially enlarged view, (c) Digital photographs of NCDs-tris-HCl-Fe3+ mixtures, (d) Light scattering experiments from Tyndall effect of different solutions.
QE of Fe3+ and Hg2+ on NCDs in water, tris-HCl (pH 7.4), NaAc-HAc (pH 5.0) and NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 (pH 7.4) buffer systems.
(a) Simulated fluorescence curves and geometrical structures of IPCA and IPCA-Fe(OH)3, (b) HUMO and LUMO of IPCA and IPCA-Fe(OH)3.
Sensing mechanism of NCDs to Fe3+.
Influence of pH, temperature and concentration of NCDs on detection range and LOD.
Performance comparison of newly reported carbon dot-based Fe3+ fluorescent probes.
Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Large-scale direct pyrolysis synthesis of excitation-independent carbon dots and analysis of ferric (III) ion sensing mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental & Catalytic Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou Normal University, Zhengzhou 450044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental & Catalytic Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou Normal University, Zhengzhou 450044, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A one-step direct pyrolysis method without any post-treatment steps and a mass yield of up to 69% was realized for the preparation of N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The NCDs exhibit highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+, and the sensing mechanism is a dynamic quenching electron transfer process in the action of hydrogen bonding and colloidal adsorption. Buffer solution plays a key role in the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid and the selective detection of Fe3+.

A one-step direct pyrolysis method without any post-treatment steps and a mass yield of up to 69% was realized for preparing N-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The NCDs exhibit excellent excitation-independent blue fluorescence with quantum yield (QY) of 65.5%, and their luminescence mechanism was explored based on optical properties and structural composition, which mainly originates from surface state photoluminescence (PL). The NCDs display highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ in tris-HCl buffer system, a good linear relationship between PL intensity ratios and Fe3+concentrations was obtained in concentration range of 0 to 400 μM with a low LOD of 0.703 μM. The formation of ferric hydroxide colloid is the main reason for the specific recognition of Fe3+ by NCDs, and buffer solution plays a key role in the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid and selective detection of Fe3+. The sensing mechanism is a dynamic quenching electron transfer process in the action of hydrogen bonding and colloidal adsorption. Three real water samples were applied to evaluate actual application of NCDs probe, satisfactory recoveries (93.5%–105.6%) indicate that NCDs has great practical potential for detection of metal ions in environmental water assays.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is well known to be one of the most abundant metals on Earth and plays a vital role in biological systems. As an indispensable trace element of the human body, iorn can improve the body's immunity, constitute hemoglobin, promote oxygen transport, and serve as a catalyst in oxidoreductase reactions [1–3]. The deficiency of iron will lead to fatigue and decreased immunity, and induce anemia. Conversely, excessive iron in human body will result in organ dysfunction, Alzheimer’s Parkinson's and other neurogenerative serious diseases [2,4]. Mild acute poisoning will occur when the serum iron concentrations rise to 300–500 μg/dL (45–72 μM), and death is common when the levels exceeds 1000 μg/dL (greater than160 μM) [5,6]. For these reasons, the detection and health monitoring of iron has always been a concern for researchers.

Up to now, various analytical methods have been established to detect Fe3+, including spectrophotometry [7], inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8], electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy [9], atomic absorption spectrometry [10], electrochemical method [11] and colorimetric analysis [12]. These analytical techniques are employed to detect Fe3+ with good selectivity, high sensitivity and excellent detection accuracy and so on. However, costly instruments, time consuming and laborious procedures for sample preparation make them limited in practical applications. In comparison, fluorescent spectrometry has received ever-increasing attention due to its high sensitivity, fast response, facile operation and cost-effectiveness, which can meet the growing demand for fast and sensitive detection of Fe3+ [13,14]. Developing new fluorescent chemosensors for Fe3+ detection with excellent sensitivity, low cost and convenient synthesis have become an urgent need.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new member of the family of fluorescent nanomaterials. Thanks to the excellent characteristics such as adjustable PL, good water solubility, low toxicity, low cost, and excellent stability, CDs have been extensively utilized in sensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, microfluidics marker, LEDs, capacitors, photovoltaic devices, and other fields [15–17]. To date, various methods and materials have been used to synthesize CDs [18,19], especially citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors, and N-doped CDs with high quantum yield were obtained [20–22]. However, most of the reported preparation methods are hydrothermal methods or other solution synthesis routes, which usually require centrifugal separation and dialysis, or filtration, precipitation and drying operations [23–25]. Sometimes it is even necessary to repeat these steps, resulting in lower yields. For this reason, this paper proposed a very simple CDs synthesis method, which still used CA and EDA as materials, but didn’t require impurity removal and drying in the solution stage, and the materials were directly converted into solid products. This method has incomparable superiority over other methods in operation, material conversion and yield, and truly realizes green economic synthesis.

The sensing of Fe3+ by CDs is not a new topic, and there are many studies on its sensing mechanism [26]. Most reports proposed a static quenching mechanism, that is, non-radiative complexes are formed between CDs and Fe3+, which lead to fluorescence quenching of CDs [27,28]. Another generally accepted mechanism is that Fe3+ collides with excited CDs to realize the electron transfer from CDs to Fe3+, which reduces the fluorescence lifetime of CDs and causes quenching [29]. Although these sensing mechanisms have been extensively studied, the fact that CDs can specifically recognize Fe3+ still lacks sufficient experimental evidence and systematic research. Zhu et al. proposed that Fe3+ could coordinate with the hydroxyl groups on the GQD surface to form aggregates such as iron hydroxide, thus exhibiting good selectivity [30], but did not provide evidence of iron hydroxide formation. Additionally, it has also been reported that Fe3+ can cause CDs aggregation and quench fluorescence [31,32]. However, the reason why Fe3+ makes CDs aggregation has not been studied in detail.

Herein, a direct pyrolysis method with a mass yield of 69% was reported to prepare fluorescent NCDs. To the best of our knowledge, the method reported here is one of the simplest and most convenient methods for large-scale, high-yield preparation of highly fluorescent CDs. The NCDs exhibit highly selective for Fe3+ detection, and the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid was proposed to be the main reason for the specific recognition of Fe3+ by NCDs, which was verified by experimental results and theoretical calculation. As far as we know, this is the first report on the effect of ferric hydroxide colloid on the fluorescence of NCDs. Buffer solution is found to play a key role in the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid and the detection of Fe3+. Moreover, the PL mechanism and formation process of NCDs were analyzed and discussed.

Analytical grade ethylenediamine (EDA) and citric acid monohydrate (CA) were provided by Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) was purchased from Aladdin chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), the other chemicals were obtained from commercial suppliers, and all the chemicals used in this work were used directly untreated. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was used for all experiments.

NCDs were prepared via one-step direct pyrolysis method without any post-treatment steps. In the typical synthesis procedure, CA (10.507 g) was dissolved in a beaker by water (20 mL), EDA (3.35 mL) was then added. The mixture solution was placed in a 120 °C oven and heated in air for 48 h. In the reaction process, the colorless solution gradually turned to orange-yellow solid, suggesting the formation of NCDs. The obtained powder was collected for direct application without any post-processing steps, and the mass yield was ca. 69%.
In order to study the effect of reaction time on the formation of NCDs, we prepared the samples with reaction times of 24 h and 72 h, named NCDs-24 and NCDs-72, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of the sample was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2100 JEOL, Akishimashi, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the sample was obtained on a Ultima Ⅳ diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were analyzed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, New York, NY, USA). IR spectra were measured on Spectrum BX spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on Cary 5000 spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Fluorescence analysis was performed on LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Transient fluorescence spectra were measured on an FLS980 spectrofluorometer (EI, Edinburgh, UK) under an excitation of 350 nm. Mass spectrum was measured on a QTrap 6500 Triple quadrupole tandem mass spectrometer (AB SCIEX, USA).

Geometric structures of the ground state have been optimized in water solvent within density functional theory (DFT) at the B3LYP /6-31G(d, p) level, and time-based density functional theory (TDDFT) has been used to compute the vertical emission energy as well as optimize the first excited electronic state using B3LYP functional combined with 6-31G(d, p) basis sets [20]. All calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 program suite.

For the detections of Fe3+, as a typical procedure, 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution was added to 1 mL of NCDs solutions to form a mixture, and then different volumes of standard Fe3+ solution (5 mM) were added. All the mixed solutions were diluted to 4 mL with ultrapure water and incubated for 10 min to record the fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex = 350 nm).

For selectivity of Fe3+ ions, metal ions (Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+) were used as interfered ions to investigate the selectivity of NCDs. A typical analysis procedure is to mix 500 μL metal ion standard solutions (5 mM) with 1 mL of NCDs dispersion (0.05 mg mL−1) and 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.4), the mixtures were incubated for 10 min for fluorescence measurements, respectively.
For stability of NCDs solution to oxidants, three common oxidants (Cr2O7 2-, MnO4 - and H2O2) were selected for analysis. The analysis procedure is the same as that for the selectivity of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Three different water samples (river water, lake water, and tap water) were used to evaluate the practical application of Fe3+ detection in real samples. The river water was taken from the Yellow River (Zhengzhou, China), and the lake water came from the Swan Lake in Zhengzhou Normal University. All water samples were diluted 10-fold after filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane. The diluted water samples as the solvents were used to prepare Tris-HCl buffer solutions. The subsequent analysis procedure is the same as that described above for the detection of Fe3+.

Fig. 1
a is the TEM image of NCDs, it is clear that the samples were uniformly dispersed without obvious aggregation. The particle size distribution shown in Fig. 1 b exhibit a Gaussian fit curve in the range of 5–25 nm, and its average particle size is 12.13 nm. The NCDs display obviously larger average diameters than the most reported CDs [33,34], which may be ascribed to the abundant functional groups present on the surface of NCDs. Weak fringes with an interval of 0.34 nm can be found in the HRTEM image of NCDs (Fig. 1 c), corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of graphite. The XRD pattern of NCDs (Fig. 1 d) displays three broad and weak peaks, the peaks at around 25.5° and 40° can be attributed to the (002) and (100) crystal planes of graphite, respectively [35], while the peak at around 20° can be assigned to the amorphous structure of carbon [28]. These results demonstrate that CA and EDA were successfully carbonized to form the carbon core of NCDs with graphite microstructure.

XPS spectra of NCDs were measured to confirm the chemical composition. The survey scans of NCDs (Fig. 2
a) show three typical peaks at 285.23, 400.32 and 531.95 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, and their contents are 59.36%, 15.10% and 25.54 mol%, respectively. The C 1 s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2 b) can be fitted into four peaks, corresponding to –COOH (288.3 eV), N–C = O/C = N (286.9 eV), C–N/C–C (sp3, 285.9 eV) and C–C/C = C (graphite, 284.8 eV), respectively [20,36,37]. The presence of characteristic peak of C–C/C = C further indicates the existence of graphite structure in the NCDs. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2 c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks, which can be assigned to conjugate C = N/O = C–NH– (399.8 eV), C–N (pyrrolic N) (400.2 eV,) and graphitic N (401.2 eV), respectively [33,38]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2 d) can be fitted into three peaks, which can be attributed to the oxygen states of –NH–C = O (531.3 eV), C = O (532.2 eV) and C–O–H (533.0 eV) [20].

FTIR spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 3 ) displays characteristic stretching vibration absorptions in the high frequency region corresponding to hydrogen-containing functional groups, including the O–H vibration (3370 cm−1), the N–H vibration (3259 cm−1) and C–H stretching vibration (3081 and 2943 cm−1). The characteristic IR vibrations of C = C and C = N were detected, located at 1550 and 1649 cm−1, respectively, suggesting the formation of unsaturated aromatic rings during the pyrolysis process. The peaks located at 1401 and 1710 cm−1 can be assigned to C–N and C = O groups. Consequently, the above results confirm the existence of functional groups such as –NH–, –COOH, –OH, and aromatic ring on the NCDs surface. Plentiful hydrophilic groups will cause excellent water solubility and stability of NCDs in aqueous solution.

As shown in Fig. 4
a, two strong absorption peaks were detected in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of NCDs solution. The peak at 240 nm is attributed to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [39], and the peak at 350 nm is due to the n–π* transition of C = N or C = O [39,40]. Meanwhile, the mixture of CA and EDA show no absorption in this region, indicating that carbon dots have been formed. The NCDs solution is light yellow in daylight, while exhibit brilliant blue light under 365 nm UV lamp (insets of Fig. 4 a), and this blue light emission can even be observed under sunlight ( Fig. S1 ), indicating that NCDs have excellent luminescence performance. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of NCDs was analyzed according to the method shown in the Supplementary Material. The calculated QY is 65.5% under the excitation of 360 nm ( Fig. S2 ), such a high PL QY is due to the introduction of N atoms to form new surface states (C–N and C = N).

The PL excitation spectrum of NCDs (λ em = 450 nm) shown in Fig. 4 a also appears two prominent peaks at 220–250 and 350 nm, agreeing well with its UV–vis spectrum, which implies that non-radiative transition scarcely occurred in NCDs. Fig. 4 b is the detailed PL emission spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelengths. Apparently, as the excitation wavelength was increased from 230 nm to 430 nm, the PL intensity of N-CQDs first increased, then decreased, and then increased again, eventually forming two plateaus at λex of 250 nm and 350 nm. This change is consistent with the trend of PL excitation spectrum. Meanwhile, it can also be found that the maximum emission peak at 450 nm did not shift with the increase of excitation wavelength, indicating excitation-independent emission properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and PL mechanism of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: NCDs exhibit excellent excitation-independent luminescence performance, indicating that there may be strong fluorescent chromophore present in NCDs, which is mainly responsible for the fluorescent properties of NCDs [41,42]. Yang’s group reported a highly fluorescent molecule, IPCA (imidazo-[1,2-a]pyridine-7-carboxylic acid, 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxo-), which was produced during heating of the mixture of CA and EDA [43]. The IPCA molecule has the fluorescence peak at 442 nm with a highly QY (85.84%) and its characteristic absorption peaks are located at 240 and 350 nm [41,43,44]. Accordingly, we expected that the IPCA molecule was also produced during the preparation of NCDs according to their optical property.

To prove the existence of IPCA, we extracted the NCDs samples with ethyl acetate to purify IPCA according to the method shown in the Supplementary Material. Fortunately, a product with a mass yield of about 3% was obtained after rotary evaporation of the extract. Fig. S3
a shows its UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectrum. The PL emission peak at 442 nm and the UV absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm are exactly the same as the peaks of IPCA reported in the literature [43]. The QY of the extract was calculated to be 81.5% based on its PL spectrum under the excitation of 360 nm ( Fig. S3
b), which is very close to that of IPCA [43]. In addition, the mass spectrum of the sample was analyzed and shown in Fig. S4 , presence of strong peaks corresponding to the molecular weights of IPCA and its fragments in the mass spectrum of the extract clearly confirm that IPCA is the major molecular structure existed in NCDs.

In order to further study the formation and PL mechanism of NCDs, we investigated the effect of reaction time on the QYs of samples. NCDs-24 and NCDs-72 are samples obtained at the reaction time of 24 h and 72 h, respectively. Quantum yield analysis spectra are shown in Fig. S3
b, it is obvious that PL intensity of samples decreased with increasing reaction time. The calculated QY of NCDs-24, NCDs and NCDs-72 were 71.1%, 65.5% and 64.8%, respectively. The QY of NCDs-72 is very close to that of NCDs prepared at reaction time of 48 h, indicating that the reaction time in the experiment (48 h) has been enough to convert the materials into NCDs. The QY of NCDs-24 is obviously lower than that of IPCA (81.5%) and higher than that of NCDs (65.5%). These results suggest that IPCA molecules were formed at the early stage of the reaction. As the reaction continues, high-QY IPCA molecules were gradually consumed due to polymerization, resulting in a decrease in QY.

It is known that the size effect from various carbon core and different surface state often cause excitation-dependent PL [42,45,46]. While NCDs exhibit excitation-independent PL, indicating that there should be only one major fluorescent active center in NCDs, which should not be derived from the carbon core, but may originate from the identical surface state or the same fluorescent molecule. According to the above results, IPCA is the important fluorescent molecule present in NCDs, but its PL emission peak (λ em = 442 nm) is different from that of NCDs (λ em = 450 nm), indicating that free IPCA molecules are not the main source of luminescence of NCDs. Since IPCA is the main product in the early stage of the formation of NCDs, in the process of gradually carbonizing to form carbon core, a large amount of IPCA can be modified to form a surface luminescence state by self-passivation on the surface of carbon core, becoming the mainly PL center of NCDs.

Based on the above analysis, the possible formation mechanism of NCDs can be proposed and shown in Scheme 1 . The carboxyl group of CA and the amino group of EDA first underwent dehydration condensation to form a mixture, which was then pyrolyzed to produce the fluorescent molecule IPCA. As the reaction continues, polymerization reaction occurs, and part of IPCA was converted into polymer clusters, which were further carbonized to generate carbon core. The IPCA molecules in the sample can be modified to the surface of carbon core by surface passivation, thereby forming surface molecular state, and eventually forming NCDs. The surface molecular state is mainly responsible for the excitation-independent luminescence of the NCDs, which could be due to the energy transfer from the carbon core to the surface fluorophores.

The stability is an important factor in the practical application of fluorescent probes, so the stability of NCDs solids and NCDs solutions are studied separately. Fig. 5
a shows the PL intensities of solutions prepared by NCDs solids stored at different temperatures for one week. Obviously, storage temperature has no observable effect on the PL intensity of NCDs, indicating that NCDs solids are stable to the storage temperature, which can be understood from the fact that they were prepared at 120℃. Fig. 5 b displays the PL intensities of solutions prepared by NCDs solids stored for different times at 25℃. It is found that the PL intensity of the solution prepared by the NCDs solid stored for one year has no significant change. The above results suggest that temperature and storage time have no effect on the fluorescence of NCDs. However, NCDs solids were found to be sensitive to air humidity. When the relative humidity of the air is greater than 70%, that is, the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor in the air to the saturated vapor pressure of water vapor at that temperature is greater than 70%, NCDs solids will absorb water and gradually deliquescence, which can be clearly observed from Fig. S5
a. Because the samples contain different moisture, the PL intensity of the NCDs solutions with the same mass concentration gradually decreases ( Fig. S5
b). Therefore, NCDs solids should be kept tightly closed to avoid exposure to high concentrations of water vapor.

The influence of pH on PL intensity of NCDs solutions was investigated. HCl (6 M or 0.06 M) and NaOH (6 M or 0.06 M) solutions were used to adjust the pH of NCDs solutions between 1.02 and 11.93. As shown in Fig. 6
a, the intensity of the emission peak at 450 nm is almost constant in pH range of 5–10, while it decreases sharply at higher or lower pH. Good fluorescent stability of NCDs over a wide pH range suggests that they are appropriate for various practical applications.

Fig. 6 b shows the relationship between PL intensity ratio F/F0 and storage time, F0 is the initial PL intensity and F is the PL intensity of the NCDs solution after storage for a certain time. As seen, there is a decrease of 3.2% in PL intensity of NCDs after seven days, which indicates that NCDs solution is photobleached, but its fluorescence decay is low, indicating that the fluorescence of NCDs is relatively stable. However, long-term storage will cause the solution to gradually turn yellow and reduce the PL intensity sharply ( Fig. S6 ), which may be due to the light and/or oxygen instability of IPCA [43].

In order to study the stability of NCDs solution to oxidizing ions or substances, three common oxidants such as Cr2O7 2-, MnO4 - and H2O2 were selected for analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 c, even in the neutral Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH 7.4) system, Cr2O7 2- and MnO4 - almost completely quench the fluorescence of NCDs, and the quenching efficiency of H2O2 at the same concentration also reaches over 85%. The quenching phenomenon can be easily observed through digital photographs (the inset of Fig. 6 c). The results show that the NCDs solution is easy to be oxidized to cause fluorescence quenching, which may be related to IPCA in the composition [43].

In addition, the salt effect of NCDs on fluorescence performance was also investigated. It is clear from Fig. 6 d that the NCDs exhibit good stability to NaCl solutions, even at a high NaCl concentration of 1 mol L-1, the PL intensity is very close to that of the blank solution (DI water), indicating high stability of the NCDs even under extreme ionic-strength conditions.

Taking the results above, these findings indicate that NCDs solid is more stable, while NCDs solutions can only remain stable for a limited time. Therefore, NCDs solution is not suitable for long-term storage.

Several common metal ions (Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, K+, Na+, Zn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ni2+) were used as interfered ions to evaluate the selectivity of NCDs. Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM, pH 7.4) was utilized to eliminate the effect of pH variation on selectivity. The emission spectra after adding various metal ions are shown in Fig. 7
a. It is obvious that the PL intensity at 450 nm is severely quenched by Fe3+, while the emission spectra of other metal ions don’t change significantly. The digital photograph of blue emissions of NCDs solutions upon addition of metal ions under 365 nm UV lamp is shown as the inset in Fig. 7 a, which clearly displays that except Fe3+, no other metal ions exhibit significant change in blue emission, agreeing with the observation noted in fluorescence spectral change.

Fig. 7 b shows the PL intensity of NCDs solutions at 450 nm in the presence of various metal ions and the coexistence of various metal ions and Fe3+. It can be seen that Fe3+ shows the most striking reduction in PL intensity of NCDs, indicating that NCDs have high selectivity for Fe3+ compared to other metals. In addition, further addition of Fe3+ into the above mixtures (Fig. 7 b, blue column) cause sharp decrease in PL intensity, which indicates that NCDs can effectively sense Fe3+ regardless of the presence of competitive metal ions, confirming that other metal ions will not interfere in the recognition of Fe3+. These results demonstrate that NCDs can be utilized as a fluorescence probe for detecting Fe3+.

Fig. 8
a shows fluorescence spectra of NCDs solutions at Fe3+ concentration range of 0 to 5000 μM. Obviously, as the Fe3+ concentration was increased, the maximum emission intensity at 450 nm gradually decreased. A good linear relationship was obtained between the PL intensity ratio (F0-F)/F and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–350 μM (Fig. 8 b). The limit of detection (LOD) can be estimated using the formula LOD = 3σ/s, where σ and s stand for standard deviation of eleven blank signal and slope of the fitting line, respectively [47]. The calculated LOD under the conditions shown in Fig. 8 b is 0.797 μM.

In order to optimize the analysis conditions, the influence of pH, temperature and concentration of NCDs on linear range and LOD were investigated. The results are listed in Table 1 , and the corresponding linear relationship diagrams are shown in Fig. S7–
Fig. S9 . It is evident from Fig. S7 that as the pH of the solutions was increased, the linear detection range first increased and then remained basically unchanged. When the pH is 7.4, the linear range is the largest. It is also found from Table 1 that there is little difference in LODs at various pH values, and the lowest value appears at pH 7.4. Therefore, it can be determined that pH 7.4 is the most suitable.

Temperature has a greater impact on detection, as shown in Fig. S8
. When the experimental temperature was increased, the linear range decreased. There is the largest detection range (0–350 μM) and the lowest LOD (0.797 μM) at room temperature (25 ℃). When the temperature is 45 ℃, the linear relationship is the worst, and the regression coefficient is only 0.98. Nevertheless, a high regression coefficients (0.99892) can be obtained at 37 ℃ at the cost of reducing the detection range, indicating that NCDs can be applied normally at human body temperature. The concentration of NCDs has the most regular effect on the detection parameters. It is clear from Fig. S9 and Table 1 that as the concentration was increased, the linear range first increased and then decreased, while the LOD first decreased and then increased. Therefore, the optimal detection conditions are NCDs concentration of 0.05 mg mL−1, a solution pH of 7.4 and a temperature of 25 ℃. At this time, the linear range is 0–400 μM, and the LOD is 0.703 μM.

Although various factors can affect the detection of Fe3+ by NCDs, highest LOD (Table 1) is only 1.298 μM, which is far below the maximum allowable limit of Fe3+ in drinking water (0.3 ppm, ~5.357 μM) specified by World Health Organization and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [48], and also far less the lowest dangerous concentration of Fe3+ in human serum.

Table 2 lists the performance comparisons of the newly reported carbon dot-based Fe3+ fluorescent probes. The LOD of this method is comparable to other nanoprobes, and more importantly, this method has a widest detection range, which is relatively rare in other methods. In addition, the simplicity and convenience of this synthesis method makes the synthesized NCDs more competitive in practical application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of storage conditions on selectivity and sensitivity

ce:section.ce:para:
Storage conditions are important factors that have an impact on the performance of fluorescent probes. The effects of storage temperature and time on the selectivity and sensitivity of NCDs solutions and NCDs solids were studied respectively. In order to reduce the influence of light, all NCDs solutions were stored in brown reagent bottles. The effect of temperature on sensitivity and selectivity of NCDs solutions is shown in Fig. S10 . It can be seen that as the temperature was increased, the linear range decreased, while the corresponding LODs shown in Table S1 remains constant. All NCDs solutions show excellent selectivity to Fe3+ ions, as shown in Fig. S10
d, although their fluorescence intensity decayed greatly as the temperature was increased. The decrease in the sensitivity of NCDs solutions can be attributed to the attenuation of fluorescence intensity during storage. For NCDs solids stored under the same conditions, as shown in Fig. S11 and Table S1, the selectivity and sensitivity do not change with increasing temperature.

The influence of storage time on the sensitivity of NCDs solutions is shown in Fig. S12 . As storage time was increased, the linear range decreased, and the LODs gradually increased (Table S1). The slight attenuation of the fluorescence intensity leads to a decrease in sensitivity, but the excellent selectivity remains unchanged ( Fig. S12
d). The selectivity and sensitivity of NCDs solids are shown in Fig. S13 and Table S1, it is clear that the storage time has no effect on their selectivity and sensitivity.

In short, storage conditions have a slight effect on the sensitivity of NCDs solutions (variation of LODs from 0.700 to 0.765 μM), but do not affect the selectivity of NCDs solutions and the sensitivity and selectivity of NCDs solids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and tested stability of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The reproducibility of NCDs was evaluated by the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures of nine different NCDs solutions (0.1 mg mL−1) and the same amount of Tris-HCl and Fe3+. Every three of the nine NCDs solutions were prepared from the same sample, and the three samples were from three independent synthesis. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of nine independent fluorescence test results (
(
F 0
- F )
F
) was calculated to be 1.5%, showing that the analysis process has acceptable reproducibility. The repeatability was checked by measuring the same NCDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) 19 times. The RSD of emission peak intensity at 450 nm is 0.21%, indicating that NCDs have excellent repeatability. As discussed above, after the NCDs solution was placed in the air for a week, the emission peak intensity retained 96.8% of its initial value, and neither sensitivity nor selectivity changes significantly. Therefore, NCDs solution is suitable for routine analysis of Fe3+ in water.

Static quenching induced by the formation of ground-state complexes and dynamic quenching depending on diffusive collisions are two common types of fluorescence quenching. The transient fluorescence spectroscopy is perceived as the most prevalent means to examine whether the quenching is a static or dynamic process. Fig. 9
a shows the transient fluorescence spectroscopy of NCDs and NCDs-Fe3+ mixture, the concentration of Fe3+ in the mixture is 333 μM. The decay curves were fitted as single exponential functions, and the calculated average decay time was 14.59 ns and 6.16 ns for NCDs and NCDs-Fe3+ mixture, respectively. The significantly reduced fluorescence lifetime indicates that the fluorescence quenching of NCDs by Fe3+ is a dynamic quenching type.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to study the interaction between NCDs and Fe3+ [57], shown as following:
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where F and F0 are the PL intensity of NCDs-Fe3+ systems and NCDs solution, [Q] is the quencher (Fe3+) concentration, K sv, K q and τ 0 are the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, the bimolecular quenching rate constant, and the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs, respectively. The Stern-Volmer diagram shown in Fig. 8 b is a straight line without bending, indicating that the quenching type should be one of both dynamic quenching and static quenching. According to the fitted linear equation, K sv was estimated to be 4.35 × 103 M−1. The bimolecular quenching rate constant K q was calculated to be 2.98 × 1011 M−1 s−1 using the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs (14.59 ns) and K sv. The K q value is 10 times larger than the possible maximum value for diffusive quenching in water, near 1 × 1010 M−1 s−1 [57], which may be due to the localization of Fe3+ in the vicinity of NCDs. This means that there may be interaction between NCDs and Fe3+.

To gain insights into the mechanism, the absorption spectra of NCDs solution, Fe3+ solution, and the NCDs-Fe3+ mixture have been measured. To be consistent with transient fluorescence spectroscopy, the concentration of Fe3+ is fixed at 333 μM. As shown in Fig. 9 b, NCDs solution has two strong absorption peaks at 240 nm and 350 nm, while the Fe3+ solution has a distinct shoulder peak at about 288 nm. These characteristic absorption peaks can be found in the spectrum of NCDs-Fe3+ mixture, and no other new absorption peaks appear, suggesting that the NCDs and Fe3+ did not form a complex in the ground state. In order to better understand the interaction between Fe3+ and NCDs, we added the absorbance of the NCDs solution and the Fe3+ solution to obtain the calculated spectrum of mixture, and compare it with the actual absorption spectrum of the NCDs and Fe3+ mixture of the same concentration. If there is no interaction between NCDs and Fe3+, it can be predicted that the two spectra should coincide. However, unexpectedly, the absorption peak near 350 nm in the actual mixture spectrum of NCD and Fe3+ (the inset in Fig. 9 b) shows a 3 nm red shift relative to the calculated spectrum (from the calculated 345 nm to 348 nm), and there is no significant change in absorbance. This result indicates that there may be a weak interaction between Fe3+ and NCDs, which is much lower than the coordination bond, so no complex was formed. However, this weak interaction will lead to the weakening of the bonds within the chromophores of NCDs, thereby causing the red shift of their absorption peaks. Additionally, the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ matched little with the PL emission spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 9 b), indicating that the fluorescence quenching is not derived from fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

It was found that the color of NCDs-tris-HCl-Fe3+ mixed solutions gradually became darker with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, and when the concentration of Fe3+ exceeds 300 μM, the mixed solutions would slowly flocculate and settle in the process of being placed. As shown in Fig. 9 c, the original yellow solution became colorless after being left overnight, and brown precipitate was obtained at the bottom of the solution. This makes it easy to think of colloidal coagulation. Since the K sp of Fe(OH)3 (2.8 × 10-39) is extremely small [30], Fe3+ is easily hydrolyzed in a solution with insufficient acidity to form Fe(OH)3 colloids. Light scattering experiments from Tyndall effect were performed to study the formation of colloids, as shown in Fig. 9 d, obvious light paths were clearly visible when NCDs-tris-HCl-Fe3+ and tris-HCl-Fe3+ mixed solutions were irradiated by a laser beam, while NCDs solution did not have this phenomenon. These results confirm the existence of Fe(OH)3 colloids and also show that tris-HCl buffer is beneficial to the formation of Fe(OH)3 colloids.

Theoretical calculation on the fluorescence spectra of NCDs and NCDs-Fe(OH)3 systems was performed to reveal the essence of the weak interaction between Fe3+ and NCDs and explore the influence of ferric hydroxide on the fluorescence of NCDs. IPCA was determined as a molecular model of NCDs due to its important role in the fluorescence performance of NCDs. The vertical emission energy of the S1 → S0 transition for IPCA have been calculated to be 2.78 eV with an oscillator strengths of 0.144, which corresponds to the transition from LUMO to HUMO. The simulated fluorescence curves are shown in Fig. 11
a. The maximum emission peak (446 nm) of IPCA is in good agreement with the experimental value (442 nm), which proves the reliability of the calculation method. However, when IPCA-Fe(OH)3 was processed in the same way, the fluorescence emission peak disappears completely (Fig. 11 a), indicating that the introduction of Fe(OH)3 can indeed quench the fluorescence of NCDs.

Fig. 11 b shows the HUMO and LUMO of IPCA and IPCA-Fe(OH)3, the HOMO of IPCA is mainly distributed on the π-conjugated system of imidazole and pyridine moiety, while LUMO is localized on the π* conjugated system of the entire molecular, so the transition from LUMO to HUMO belongs to the π*→π vertical transition of the molecule, which is a process of strong fluorescence emission. S1 → S0 de-excitation process of IPCA with oscillator strength (f = 0.144) could be allotted to the LUMO → HOMO with 99% contribution. The HUMO of IPCA-Fe(OH)3 is mainly distributed in imidazole and pyridine moiety, while LUMO is only located in Fe(OH)3 moiety, which means that the electrons on fluorescent matrix can be transferred to Fe(OH)3. S1 → S0 transition allotted to the LUMO → HOMO is a forbidden transition with little oscillator strength (0.0000), causes the fluorescence to be completely quenched. Therefore, the electron transfer from IPCA to Fe(OH)3 is regarded as a photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process, and the excited electrons are prevented from returning to the original part and trigger fluorescence quenching. In addition, it is found from the structure of IPCA-Fe(OH)3 in Fig. 11 a that intermolecular hydrogen bonds were formed between Fe(OH)3 and IPCA molecule. The hydrogen bond lengths between the hydrogen atom of Fe(OH)3 and the carbonyl oxygen of carboxyl group and the nitrogen atom of the imidazole are 2.00 Å and 2.19 Å, respectively. The vertical distance of 3.33 Å shows that there is no bond formation between Fe3+ and IPCA molecules. In general, the theoretical calculation results show that iron hydroxide can cause the fluorescence quenching of NCDs, which is achieved through the electron transfer process. The hydrogen bond is the essence of the weak interaction between NCDs and Fe3+.

Based on the above results, we can propose a possible sensing mechanism. As shown in Scheme 2 , the strong hydrolysis ability of Fe3+ makes it mainly exist in the form of Fe(OH)3 colloid in tris-HCl system. The hydrogen bonding and colloidal adsorption make NCDs aggregate in the vicinity of Fe(OH)3 colloidal particles, which causes the bimolecular quenching rate constant to be increased. When NCDs were excited by light and transformed into the excited state, the electron on the excited NCDs can be easily transferred to Fe(OH)3 colloidal particles, resulting in a decrease of fluorescence lifetime, and causing the fluorescence quenching of NCDs. Therefore, the quenching mechanism between Fe3+ and NCDs is electron transfer through dynamic quenching with the combined effect of hydrogen bonding and colloidal adsorption.

Three real water samples were selected to evaluate the practical feasibility of NCDs using standard addition methods. Water samples were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and diluted to prepare buffer solution. The mixtures of NCDs solution and buffer solution were spiked with Fe3+ ions of different known concentrations for detection of PL intensity. The concentrations of Fe3+ ions in water samples were calculated based on the detected PL intensity. The experiments were repeated three times under each condition for analyzing the relative standard deviation (RSD). As shown in Table 3 , a good agreement between the measured and the spiked concentration of Fe3+ was obtained, good recoveries (93.5–105.6%) and low RSDs (0.058–0.77%) demonstrate the feasibility of the NCDs fluorescent nanoprobe for quantifying Fe3+ in real samples.

In summary, a simple, economical and scalable direct pyrolysis method was reported for preparing fluorescence NCDs using EDA and CA. The obtained NCDs exhibit excitation-independent blue-fluorescence with QY of 65.5%, and the surface molecular state is the source of fluorescence of NCDs. The NCDs can be utilized as a fluorescent probe to selectively detect Fe3+ in the wide linear range of 0 to 400 μM with a LOD as low as 0.703 μM. The fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ on NCDs is an electron transfer process through dynamic quenching. The specific recognition of Fe3+ by NCDs is attributed to colloidal adsorption of ferric hydroxide and the hydrogen bonding between NCDs and ferric hydroxide, and buffer solution plays a key role in the formation of ferric hydroxide colloid and the selective detection of Fe3+. The NCDs could also detect Fe3+ in real water samples with acceptable recoveries and low RSDs, implying the feasibility as a credible fluorescent probe for environmental Fe3+ assays.

Yongyu Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jianjun Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Yaping Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Huiji Li: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Jihong Yin: Investigation. Mengyan Li: Investigation. Ling Wang: Data curation, Conceptualization. Haijie Sun: Data curation, Project administration. Lingxia Chen: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Found of China (No. U1304204), Henan Provincial Scientific and technological projects (No. 182102210563), Young Backbone Teacher Training Program (201803) and Environmental Catalysis Innovation Team (702010) and Undergraduate Innovation Project (DCZ2019005) of Zhengzhou Normal University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.148151.

ce:section.ce:para:

A: Chem., 325 (2016) 104-110.

A: Chem., 377 (2019) 138-148.

J.R. Lakowicz, Principles of Fluorescence Spectroscopy, 3rd ed., Springer: Boston, MA, US, 2006.
